anonymized_passage: when a proven practice is transferred to a different unit, before whether the practice's mechanism survives the new context is known, a business-unit leader chooses whether and how to adapt the practice using local routines, resource limits, and observed prior outcomes. The stated objective is to improve unit performance without disrupting complementary routines, subject to the condition that the leader cannot assume that surface similarity preserves the causal mechanism. Transfer succeeds when the receiving unit recreates the complementary routines that produced the original effect. Closer imitation preserves tested elements but may fit the receiving unit poorly. The argument applies to transfers with observable routines and does not establish universal causality. The contribution is that the same topic has not previously been studied in this particular context.

model_or_study_facts: the leader cannot assume that surface similarity preserves the causal mechanism
whether the practice's mechanism survives the new context
decisions: chooses whether and how to adapt the practice
context.objective: improve unit performance without disrupting complementary routines
context.timing: when a proven practice is transferred to a different unit
decision_time_information: local routines, resource limits, and observed prior outcomes
constraints: the leader cannot assume that surface similarity preserves the causal mechanism
context.mechanism: Transfer succeeds when the receiving unit recreates the complementary routines that produced the original effect
supplied_evidence: Synthetic illustration; no external empirical evidence supplied.
